In [ ]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from wordcloud import WordCloud as wc
In [ ]:
data = pd.read_csv('/content/Datasets/Netflix_Titles Dataset/netflix_titles.csv')

Interpretation of data.info() :¶

  • total : 7787 rows and 12 columns

  • except release_year (integer type) , all the other columns are of object type .

  • Problems :

  • Date_added column : type is object , making it difficult for the date analysis in the future . Important for a time series analysis.

  • Null values : columns director , cast , rating and country contain null values , which need to be removed or corrected . Director contains the majority count of null values .

  • List item

Step 3 : Data Cleaning and Transformation :¶

GOAL :

  • To correct the columns director , cast , rating and country as they contain null values.
  • date column change for better time series operations and analysis .
In [ ]:
data.isnull().sum()
Out[ ]:
0
show_id 0
type 0
title 0
director 2389
cast 718
country 507
date_added 10
release_year 0
rating 7
duration 0
listed_in 0
description 0

In [ ]:
# 1. handling the missing in director and cast columns :
# fill those nan values with unknown because we cannot
# really the guess the names with modes or median .
# empty cells cause problems which is why we're filling them

data['cast'].fillna('unknown' , inplace = True)
data['director'].fillna('unknown' , inplace = True)

/tmp/ipykernel_2249/523429699.py:6: FutureWarning: A value is trying to be set on a copy of a DataFrame or Series through chained assignment using an inplace method.
The behavior will change in pandas 3.0. This inplace method will never work because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy.

For example, when doing 'df[col].method(value, inplace=True)', try using 'df.method({col: value}, inplace=True)' or df[col] = df[col].method(value) instead, to perform the operation inplace on the original object.


  data['cast'].fillna('unknown' , inplace = True)
/tmp/ipykernel_2249/523429699.py:7: FutureWarning: A value is trying to be set on a copy of a DataFrame or Series through chained assignment using an inplace method.
The behavior will change in pandas 3.0. This inplace method will never work because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy.

For example, when doing 'df[col].method(value, inplace=True)', try using 'df.method({col: value}, inplace=True)' or df[col] = df[col].method(value) instead, to perform the operation inplace on the original object.


  data['director'].fillna('unknown' , inplace = True)
In [ ]:
# 2. the empty values in countries can be filled with mode
# or with empty value

data['country'] = data['country'].fillna(data['country'].mode()[0])
In [ ]:
# 3. dropping the missing rows of date_added and ratings is safer option
# since the number of those is small

data.dropna(subset = ['date_added' , 'rating'] , inplace = True)
In [ ]:
# 4. convert date_added to datetype column

data['date_added'] = pd.to_datetime(data['date_added'] , format = 'mixed' , dayfirst = False )
In [ ]:
# 5. extracting the year and month in which the movie was added :

data['year_added'] = data['date_added'].dt.year
data['month_added'] = data['date_added'].dt.month

Step 4 : Exploratory Data Analysis :¶

In [ ]:
# distribution of content type ?

data['type'].value_counts()
Out[ ]:
count
type
Movie 5372
TV Show 2398

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize = (8,6))
plt.pie(data['type'].value_counts() ,
        labels = data['type'].value_counts().index ,
        autopct = '%1.1f%%',
        startangle = 140 )
plt.title('Distribution of Content Types')
plt.show()

Insights :

  • movies largely dominate the platform .
In [ ]:
# how has the content been added over time ?

content_over_time = data.groupby(['year_added' , 'type']).size().unstack().fillna(0)

plt.figure(figsize = (14,8))

content_over_time.plot(kind = 'line' , stacked = True)
plt.title('Content Added Over Time')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Number of Content over time ')
plt.show()

<Figure size 1400x800 with 0 Axes>

insight : there were huge uploads between 2017 and 2020 , a result of covid pandemic when the people were in lockdown .

Theoretical Concept: Handling Multi-Value Text Columns¶

The listed_in column contains strings with multiple genres separated by commas (e.g., "Dramas, International Movies"). To analyze each genre individually, we need to transform the data. A common technique is to:

  1. Split the string in each row into a list of genres.
  2. Explode the DataFrame so that each genre in the list gets its own row, duplicating the other information for that title. This allows us to perform a value_counts() on the genres.
In [ ]:
# most popular genres (top 15) ?

genres = data.assign(genre = data['listed_in'].str.split(', ')).explode('genre')

top_genres_count = genres['genre'].value_counts().reset_index()
top_genres_count.columns = ['Genres' , 'Count']

# top 15 :
top_genres_count.head(15)
# how has the content been added over time ?

plt.figure(figsize = (14,8))

sns.barplot(y = 'Genres' , x = 'Count' , data= top_genres_count.head(15), hue = 'Genres', weights = 'Count')
plt.title('Top Genres Count')
plt.xlabel('Count')
plt.ylabel('Genres')
plt.show()

Insights :

  • The top 5 content consists of international content , comedies , and dramas . This showcases audience's tastes reflecting in the distribution .
In [ ]:
# distribution of content duration ?

# 1. separate movies and tv shows :
movies = data[data['type'] == 'Movie']
tv_shows = data[data['type'] == 'TV Show']
In [ ]:
movies['duration'] = movies['duration'].str.replace(' min' , '').astype('int')
tv_shows['duration'] = tv_shows['duration'].str.replace(' Seasons' , '').str.replace( ' Season', '').astype('int')

/tmp/ipykernel_2249/4045936211.py:1: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  movies['duration'] = movies['duration'].str.replace(' min' , '').astype('int')
/tmp/ipykernel_2249/4045936211.py:2: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  tv_shows['duration'] = tv_shows['duration'].str.replace(' Seasons' , '').str.replace( ' Season', '').astype('int')
In [ ]:
# plotting the distribution :
fig , axes = plt.subplots(1,2,figsize = (18 ,7))

# movies duration distribution :
sns.histplot(ax = axes[0] , data = movies , x = 'duration' , bins = 50 , kde = True ).set_title('distribution of movies over duration (minutes) : ')
# tv shows disstribution :
sns.histplot(ax = axes[1] , data = tv_shows , x = 'duration' , bins = 50 , kde = True ).set_title('distribution of tv shows over duration (seasons) : ')
plt.show()

Insights :

  • majority of the movies seems to lie in the between 80 to 120 minute duration range
  • majority of the tv shows are short lived till one season .
In [ ]:
# where does the content come from ? (geographical analysis) :
# top 15 countries for that :

plt.figure(figsize = (12 , 5))
top_15_countries = data.country.value_counts().head(15).reset_index()
top_15_countries.columns = ['country' , 'count']

sns.barplot(  x = 'count' , y = 'country' ,
            data = top_15_countries , hue = 'country' ,
            legend = True ).set_title('distribution of content over countries : ')
plt.xlabel('count')
plt.ylabel('country')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • USA is the largest content producer over netflix among the top 15 countries with India following .
  • countries like UK , south korea and japan have their content distribution high up as well , showcasing their global adaptation by the netflix audience .
In [ ]:
# what are the maturity ratings of the contents ?

plt.figure(figsize = (12 , 5))
maturity_ratings = data.rating.value_counts().reset_index()
maturity_ratings.columns = ['rating' , 'count']

sns.barplot(data = maturity_ratings , x= 'count' , y = 'rating' , hue = 'rating').set_title('Rating distribution over the platform :')
plt.xlabel('count')
plt.ylabel('Ratings on Netflix')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • Most of the content uploaded on netflix lies in the category 'TV-MA' (content for Mature Audiences) meaning .

  • second highest category is 'TV-14' , for viewers of age 14 and above .

  • The top 5 categories suggest that most of the content uploaded contains violence (verbal / physical / sexual) , abuse , and content that is not suitable for viewer under 18 years of age (even if they are marked as fit for 14 and above) .

  • The categories of these contents also suggest that majority of audience lie in the age of range 15 and above , which also promotes the uploads of such content on the platform .

Step 5 : Feature Engineering¶

Let's create a new feature to analyze how old content is when it gets added to Netflix. This can tell us about their acquisition strategy (buying old classics vs. releasing new originals).

In [ ]:
# plotting the content age graph :
data['content_age'] = data['year_added'] - data['release_year']
content_age = data[data['content_age'] >= 0 ]
plt.figure(figsize = (12 ,5))

sns.histplot(data = content_age , x = 'content_age' , bins = 50 , kde = True ).set_title("How old is the content on netflix ?")
plt.xlabel('How old is the content ? ')
plt.ylabel('How many movies are in this age gorup ? ')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • majority of the content seem to have been released on netlfix or released and uploaded on netfix in the same year . This is characteristic of its 'Netflix Originals' .

  • The tail extends farther than 80 ,showing that netlfix also invest in uploading content that decades old , showing that it also stores 'classic' films and shows .

Step 6 : Deeper Multivariate Analysis¶

In [ ]:
# Movie duration across different top genres ?

top_5_genres = genres.genre.value_counts().index[:5]
top_genres_movies = genres[(genres['type'].str.lower() == 'movie') & (genres['genre'].isin(top_5_genres))].copy()
top_genres_movies['duration_min'] = top_genres_movies['duration'].str.replace(' min' , '' ).astype(int)

plt.figure(figsize = (15,8) )
sns.boxplot(data = top_genres_movies , x = 'genre' , y = 'duration_min' ,hue = 'genre' ,  ).set_title('Movie duration across different top genres')
plt.xlabel('Genres')
plt.ylabel('Duration in min')
plt.xticks(rotation = 45)
plt.show()

Insights :

  • The medians of all genres lie really around 90 to 100 minutes , indicating majority of the movies being about 100 minutes long .
  • There can be seen a lot of variations as well . Such as in dramas genre , there is a lot of content in different durations , alogn with movie like content. Similarly , International movies also contain movies of varying duration , presenting diverse filmmaking styles around the world .
In [ ]:
# How has the distribution of content ratings changed over time?


df_copy = data.copy()
# created a new column that stores decades
# instead of individual year for better understanding
df_copy['decade'] = (df_copy['release_year'] // 10 ) * 10
# grouped by decade and rating then unstacked it for visual building
rating_year = df_copy.groupby(['decade' , 'rating']).size().unstack(fill_value = 0 )

# area plot shows how much of all the elements have increased over every decade :
rating_year.plot(kind = 'area' , stacked = True , figsize = (13 ,8) )
plt.title('Distribution of Content Ratings Over Time')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Number of Content Ratings')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • Before 1980 : The ratings are really less to infer , likely because content from this time is in quantity than the newly released content on the platform .
  • After 1980 : In this time , there has been an enormous increase in the content rated as 'TV-MA' , 'TV-14' ,and 'TV-PG'. This shows that over time , content rated in this categroy were added more as compared to "R" , 'PG-13' , 'UR' , and 'NC-17' .

This shows that Netflix prioritized adding more adult rated content . They claim their is to cater to diverse audiences and promote artistic appeal .However viewers say otherwise , claiming it is unnecessary .

In [ ]:
# Is there a relationship between content age and its type (Movie vs. TV Show)?

plt.figure(figsize = (12,8))
sns.violinplot(data = data , y = 'type' , x = 'content_age')
plt.show()

Insights :

  • Teh difference of time between addition of content and its relase is not much (~0) .This implies for both movies and TV-shows .
  • We can say that newly released content is more likely to get uploaded on the Netflix platform . Specially TV-shows.
In [ ]:
# Can we identify any trends in content production
# based on the release year vs. the year added to Netflix

plt.figure(figsize = (11 , 5))
sns.scatterplot(data = data , x = 'release_year', y = 'year_added' , hue = 'type')
plt.title('Trends in Content Production Based on Release Year vs. Year Added to Netflix')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • Netflix added old content between 2016 and 2020 in bulk . Since then it has added content that was either released in the same year or recently .
  • Newer content tended to be added almost quickly after its release (in the same year or after that) .
In [ ]:
# What are the most common word pairs or phrases in content descriptions?

# would have to do a bit of NLP for that :
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer as cv

df_copy= data.copy()
desc = df_copy['description'].dropna().str.lower()

# creating bigrams (pair of two words)
vect = cv( stop_words = 'english' , ngram_range = (2,2) )
X = vect.fit_transform(desc)
sum_words = X.sum(axis=0)

words_freq = [(word, sum_words[0, idx])
              for word, idx in vect
              .vocabulary_.items()]

words_freq = sorted(words_freq, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

top_phrases_bi = words_freq[:20]

# print(top_phrases_bi)

# visualising them :

phrases = [i[0] for i in top_phrases_bi]
count = [i[1] for i in top_phrases_bi]

plt.barh(phrases , count)
plt.xlabel('frequency')
plt.title('Most common word pairs in title descriptions : ')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • Phrases like 'small town' , 'new york' , 'young woman/man' , 'year old' and 'high school' place in the top 5 . Phrases 'small town' and 'new york' signify that the majoirity content plot is usually situated in new york or some small town .
  • Phrases 'young/woman' , 'year old' , and 'high school' signify that content's plot usually caters to specific genders and age groups .

eg : 'a 20 year old young woman is determined to make a place in the bustling city of new york' This attracts 20 yo female viewers .

Therefore we can say that netflix is more aligned towards adding content that includes stories inspired by real events ,is violent ('serial killer', 'world war' , 'war ii') , has the plot based in LA or NY cities , and has characters mainly from high school . It also contains documentary films as well.

In [ ]:
#  creating trigrams (three words at a time) :
vect = cv( stop_words = 'english' , ngram_range =(3,3) )
X = vect.fit_transform(desc)
sum_words = X.sum(axis=0)

words_freq = [(word, sum_words[0, idx])
              for word, idx in vect
              .vocabulary_.items()]

words_freq = sorted(words_freq, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

top_phrases_tri = words_freq[:20]

# visualising them :

phrases = [i[0] for i in top_phrases_tri]
count = [i[1] for i in top_phrases_tri]

plt.barh(phrases , count)
plt.xlabel('frequency')
plt.title('Most common word phrases in title descriptions : ')

plt.show()

Insights :

  • Phrases like '10 year old' , '11 year old' are among the top most repeated phrases . This also shows similarities with the other phrase like '12/14/15/17 year old' . this means that most of the content includes actors in between ages 12 and 17 years old .

  • Among the top phrases also includes 'based true events/story' meaning that most of the content prodcued consists of real life stories or inspirations .

  • 'world war ii' is the most used phrases of all the phrases . This shows that content depicted times from the world war ii period .

Overall , we can conclude that most of the content includes teenage actors , real life story replication or inspiration , stories presented in the new york city or people from new york city , and hints of world war ii.

In [ ]:
# Who are the top directors on Netflix?
# 1. filtering out the 'unknown's from the director's column :
dir_graph = data[data['director'] != 'unknown'].director
dir_graph =dir_graph.value_counts().head(20).reset_index()
dir_graph.columns = ['director' , 'count']
dir_graph.head()

# 2. plotting them on a barplot :
plt.barh( dir_graph['director'] , dir_graph['count'] )

plt.show()

Insights :

  • Top five include : 'Martin Scorcese' ,'Jay Karas' ,'Marcus Raboy'(american) ,Cathy Garcia-Molina (filipino) , 'raul campos' and 'jan suter' (mexican)

This shows that content from US , mexico , and filipines present on the platform in a huge quantity as compared to the other nation films . This could also show that these directors are a part of multiple content at the same time

Step 7 : Word Cloud from Content Descriptions¶

Generating a word cloud for a visual analysis from the description column to see what themes and words are the most common on Netflix .

In [ ]:
text = ' '.join(data.description)
wordcloud = wc(width = 800 , height = 500 , background_color = 'white').generate(text)

# displaying the generated image :
plt.figure(figsize = (12 , 8) )
plt.imshow(wordcloud , interpolation = 'bilinear')
plt.axis('off')
plt.title('Most common words in Netflix Content descriptions : ' , fontsize = 20)
plt.show()

Insights :

  • Words like 'love' , 'life' , 'world' , family' , 'friend' were used often in content descriptions showcasing that most of the content showcases human relationships .
  • Words like 'new' , 'find' and 'help' can also be seen suggesting the inlcusion of mystery content as well .

Final Conclusion and Summary of Insights :¶

  • Content Strategy : Netflix's library is mostly movie-heavy (~70%) , and agressively added content between 2016 - 2019 . This includes majority of the content being newly released as well as classic , decades old shows and movies .

  • US's Domination : US is the largest contributor to the Netflix content database . Indian content follows .

  • Rating : The uploaded content is largely categorised and listed for mature audiences (TV-MA) and for viewers above 14 (TV-14).

  • Duration : A huge portion of the movies lie in the duration of 80 to 120 min while majority of the TV-shows contain only one season . This shows a high-risk high-reward approach to series production .

  • Theme : A large portion of the content have themes revolving aroung concepts of love , family , friendships etc .

There is a limitation : This dataset is about the supply of the content and not the popularity or demand of the content on the platform .

In [ ]:
